skill.name: webapp-testing
skill.description: Guidance and examples for testing local web applications with Python Playwright, including server startup, DOM inspection, screenshots, and console log capture.
skill.summary: Test local web applications with Playwright automation and server lifecycle helpers.
skill.author: Cam10001110101

security_audit.summary: AI review dismissed the weak cryptography and markdown backtick detections as false positives. The helper script intentionally runs local commands, including a shell-based server command, and the documentation encourages black-box script execution. Network and filesystem findings are limited to localhost browser testing and local screenshot or log output.

medium_findings.title: Intentional Local Command Execution Helper
medium_findings.description: The helper starts user-supplied server commands with shell=True and then runs a requested command. This is useful for local testing, but unsafe if command strings come from untrusted prompts, web content, or copied instructions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess calls are direct and the server command uses shell=True. The context shows a legitimate local testing helper, so the concern is controlled command execution rather than confirmed malware.

medium_findings.title: Documentation Encourages Black-Box Script Execution
medium_findings.description: The documentation tells users to run helper scripts before reading their source and to use bundled scripts as black boxes. This can reduce review before executing community code.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The wording is explicit and appears in operational instructions. It does not claim system authority or ask to skip security analysis, so it is a moderate safety concern rather than a critical prompt injection.

low_findings.title: Localhost URLs in Browser Testing Examples
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to localhost for local web application testing. No evidence found of external data exfiltration or contact with third-party services.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All cited URLs use localhost and match the stated purpose of testing local applications. This makes the static network findings likely benign.

low_findings.title: Local Screenshot and Log File Writes
low_findings.description: Examples write console logs and screenshots to local output paths. This is expected for debugging, but users should avoid capturing sensitive screens or logs unnecessarily.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are visible and limited to debugging artifacts. They create privacy and overwrite considerations, but no hidden persistence or exfiltration was found.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Cryptography Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The lines flagged for weak cryptography are argument definitions, skill description text, and selector guidance. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or other weak cryptographic use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines do not perform cryptography and only contain help text or documentation. This strongly supports a false-positive classification.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Command Detections Are Documentation
low_findings.description: Most external command detections in SKILL.md are markdown code spans or example commands. They document expected local development workflows rather than hidden execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged backticks are rendered documentation and are not executable code by themselves. They still describe command usage, so they are tracked as low-risk context.

dangerous_patterns.title: subprocess.Popen with shell=True
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper uses shell=True to support compound server commands. This should only be used with commands supplied by the local developer.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact subprocess pattern is present and shell=True is explicit. The legitimate purpose is clear, but the execution primitive remains sensitive.

dangerous_patterns.title: Arbitrary Command Runner
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper executes the remaining command arguments after servers start. This is intentional, but it expands the skill into general local command execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command execution is direct and visible. It is not shell-expanded at this call site, which lowers injection risk but not execution impact.

content.user_title: Test Local Web Apps with Playwright
content.value_statement: Local web app testing often needs browser automation, screenshots, and console logs. This skill guides Playwright scripts and server lifecycle helpers for repeatable frontend checks.
actual_capabilities: Guides Python Playwright scripts for headless Chromium testing.
Shows how to start one or more local servers before automation.
Captures browser screenshots for visual inspection and debugging.
Captures console messages from a page during browser automation.
Discovers rendered buttons, links, and form inputs for selector planning.
Explains when to inspect static HTML versus rendered dynamic pages.
limitations: It focuses on local web applications, not hosted production monitoring.
It requires Playwright and browser dependencies to be installed.
Server commands run locally and must be reviewed before use.
It provides examples and workflow guidance, not a full test framework.

use_cases.title: Debug a Local UI Flow
use_cases.description: Run a local app, open it in Chromium, inspect the rendered page, and verify basic user interactions.

use_cases.title: Collect Evidence for a Bug Report
use_cases.description: Capture screenshots, console logs, and visible page state from a reproducible local browser session.

use_cases.title: Explore Selectors Before Automation
use_cases.description: Discover buttons, links, inputs, and rendered content before writing a reliable browser automation script.

prompt_templates.title: Open a Local App
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner check of a running frontend server.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to open my local web app at port 5173, wait for the page to load, and summarize visible issues.

prompt_templates.title: Capture Console Logs
prompt_templates.scenario: Debug browser console errors during one interaction.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Playwright to visit my local app, capture console messages, click the main dashboard action, and report any errors.

prompt_templates.title: Start Server and Test Flow
prompt_templates.scenario: Run automation after starting a development server.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the server command first, then use the helper to start my app and verify the login-to-dashboard flow with Playwright.

prompt_templates.title: Investigate Dynamic UI State
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced troubleshooting of rendered selectors and timing.
prompt_templates.prompt: Inspect the rendered DOM after network idle, identify stable selectors, run the target interaction, and provide screenshots plus findings.

output_examples.input: Test the local app homepage and report visible browser errors.
output_examples.output: A concise report listing page load status, console errors, screenshot location, and recommended fixes for failed UI states.

output_examples.input: Find clickable controls on a rendered dashboard page.
output: Summary of discovered buttons and links.
Notes about hidden or ambiguous controls.
Suggested selectors for the next automation step.

output_examples.input: Run a local server and verify a signup workflow.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step test result covering server readiness, browser actions, observed page changes, and any failing assertion.

best_practices: Review local server commands before running helper scripts.
Wait for network idle before inspecting dynamic application state.
Use stable role, text, ID, or CSS selectors for repeatable tests.
anti_patterns: Do not run server commands copied from untrusted web pages.
Do not inspect a dynamic app before JavaScript has finished loading.
Do not save screenshots or logs that expose sensitive user data.

faq.question: Does this skill test production websites?
faq.answer: It is designed for local web applications and development servers. Production testing needs separate safety and access controls.

faq.question: Which browser automation library does it use?
faq.answer: It uses Python Playwright examples with headless Chromium for local browser automation.

faq.question: Can it start my frontend server?
faq.answer: Yes. The helper can start one or more local server commands before running an automation command.

faq.question: Is command execution required?
faq.answer: Command execution is needed when starting local servers or running automation scripts. Review commands before running them.

faq.question: Can it capture screenshots and logs?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples show screenshot capture and browser console log collection for debugging.

faq.question: Does it create full test suites?
faq.answer: No. It provides workflows and examples that help create focused Playwright checks.
